raw: {"weights":{"R1.1":0.3,"R1.2":0.7,"R1.3":1.0,"R1.4":0.7,"R1.5":0.0,"R1.6":0.3,"R1.7":0.7,"RX.1":1.0,"RX.2":1.0,"RX.3":1.0,"RX.4":1.0,"RX.5":1.0},"active_set":["R1.2","R1.3","R1.4","R1.7","RX.1","RX.2","RX.3","RX.4","RX.5"],"rationale":{"R1.2":"Multiple internal consistency failures demand scrutiny: COCO-Stuff is used for both semantic segmentation (47.9 mIoU) and panoptic quality (49.3 PQ) but the two tasks and metrics are conflated as mutually reinforcing; 'strongest unified segmentation model' is asserted despite only matching (not surpassing) SOTA on Pascal-Context and providing no SOTA comparison on COCO-Stuff semantic; the ablation reference to Section 5 creates a cross-section dependency that must hold.","R1.3":"Load-bearing: the entire credibility of the results section rests on specific numerical deltas (SegMaster 84.2 vs. InternImage-H 83.5; 56.1 vs. 55.3) and the 1.2 mIoU average ablation drop. Any misalignment between these reported numbers and actual experimental outputs invalidates the section. The Pascal-Context result ('matches' rather than surpasses) is in tension with the SOTA narrative and needs explicit reconciliation.","R1.4":"Baseline selection is central to the novelty claim. InternImage-H and ViT-Adapter must be the true SOTA comparators at submission time for their respective benchmarks. No competitor is named for COCO-Stuff semantic, leaving the 47.9 mIoU claim without a novelty anchor. The panoptic PQ of 49.3 similarly cites no baseline, so its framing as 'consistent' with segmentation results is unverifiable.","R1.7":"Three specific attribution obligations: (1) the published scores for InternImage-H (83.5) and ViT-Adapter (55.3) must match their source papers; (2) the plug-compatibility claim for Swin-V2 and InternImage requires a citation or experimental basis; (3) the medical imaging future-work sentence invokes a body of prior work ('similar refinement strategies have shown promise') without any reference, which is an unsupported scholarly claim."},"clarification_needed":true,"clarification_question":"Does 'solid' mean an evidence-and-logic audit—verifying numerical claims, SOTA baseline accuracy, and internal consistency—or a prose-quality review focused on sentence clarity, narrative flow, and writing polish?"}

rationale.R1.2: Multiple internal consistency failures demand scrutiny: COCO-Stuff is used for both semantic segmentation (47.9 mIoU) and panoptic quality (49.3 PQ) but the two tasks and metrics are conflated as mutually reinforcing; 'strongest unified segmentation model' is asserted despite only matching (not surpassing) SOTA on Pascal-Context and providing no SOTA comparison on COCO-Stuff semantic; the ablation reference to Section 5 creates a cross-section dependency that must hold.
rationale.R1.3: Load-bearing: the entire credibility of the results section rests on specific numerical deltas (SegMaster 84.2 vs. InternImage-H 83.5; 56.1 vs. 55.3) and the 1.2 mIoU average ablation drop. Any misalignment between these reported numbers and actual experimental outputs invalidates the section. The Pascal-Context result ('matches' rather than surpasses) is in tension with the SOTA narrative and needs explicit reconciliation.
rationale.R1.4: Baseline selection is central to the novelty claim. InternImage-H and ViT-Adapter must be the true SOTA comparators at submission time for their respective benchmarks. No competitor is named for COCO-Stuff semantic, leaving the 47.9 mIoU claim without a novelty anchor. The panoptic PQ of 49.3 similarly cites no baseline, so its framing as 'consistent' with segmentation results is unverifiable.
rationale.R1.7: Three specific attribution obligations: (1) the published scores for InternImage-H (83.5) and ViT-Adapter (55.3) must match their source papers; (2) the plug-compatibility claim for Swin-V2 and InternImage requires a citation or experimental basis; (3) the medical imaging future-work sentence invokes a body of prior work ('similar refinement strategies have shown promise') without any reference, which is an unsupported scholarly claim.

parsed.clarification_question: Does 'solid' mean an evidence-and-logic audit—verifying numerical claims, SOTA baseline accuracy, and internal consistency—or a prose-quality review focused on sentence clarity, narrative flow, and writing polish?
